Sealing is **mandatory**: if the app cannot be sealed into a valid image, the deploy fails rather than shipping a bundle that cold-compiles on the pod's first boot. When a pod starts, the compiler auto-loads the sibling `_precompiled/` image, so services run from precompiled modules with no on-pod compile step - for both single-app and microservice deployments.

If a module in your project cannot be sealed (for example, a file that fails to compile), the deploy aborts with the seal error. Fix or exclude the offending module and redeploy.

### Naming & Namespace

Controls the application name used for all Kubernetes resource names and the namespace resources are created in.

| TOML Key  | Default | Description |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| `app_name` | slug of `[project].name` | Prefix for all K8s resource names (deployments, services, secrets, etc.). Falls back to `jaseci` when no project name is usable |
| `namespace`| `default` | Kubernetes namespace to deploy into |

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
app_name = "myapp"
namespace = "production"
```

### Ports

Controls how the application is exposed inside the cluster and externally.

By default, jac-scale deploys a **dedicated NGINX Ingress controller per app**. The controller listens on one NodePort and routes requests to the correct ClusterIP service based on path. Individual services (app, Grafana, dashboards) are all ClusterIP and not directly reachable from outside the cluster.

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `container_port`| `8000` | Port your app listens on inside the pod |
| `ingress_node_port` | `30080` | NodePort for the NGINX Ingress controller (all external traffic enters here) |

| Path | Destination |
|------|-------------|
| `http://localhost:30080/` | Jaseci application |
| `http://localhost:30080/grafana` | Grafana dashboard (if monitoring enabled) |
| `http://localhost:30080/cache-dashboard/` | Redis Insight (if `redis_dashboard = true`) |
| `http://localhost:30080/db-dashboard` | Mongo Express (if `mongodb_dashboard = true`) |

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
container_port = 8000
ingress_node_port = 30080
```

### Shared Ingress

By default each app deploys its own NGINX controller (one Deployment, one NodePort/NLB, one IngressClass). Set `shared_ingress = true` to skip that and attach the app's routing rules to a pre-existing shared NGINX controller in your cluster instead.

- You already run a cluster-wide `ingress-nginx` controller (e.g. installed via Helm) and don't want a separate controller per app
- You are deploying multiple apps to the same cluster and want to reduce resource overhead

- A running NGINX ingress controller must already exist in the cluster
- `domain` **must** be set. The shared controller sees Ingress resources from all namespaces, so host-based routing is the only way to differentiate two apps. jac-scale raises an error at deploy time if `domain` is empty when `shared_ingress = true`

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `shared_ingress` | `false` | Use a pre-existing shared controller instead of deploying a dedicated one |
| `shared_ingress_class` | `"nginx"` | IngressClass name of the shared controller |
| `shared_ingress_annotations` | `{}` | Extra annotations merged onto the Ingress. Required to drive non-nginx controllers (AWS ALB, Traefik, GKE). Caller-supplied values take precedence |
| `shared_ingress_tls` | `false` | Set when the controller terminates TLS out-of-band (e.g. ALB via an ACM cert) so the reported URL uses `https`. nginx+cert-manager (`spec.tls`) is detected automatically |

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
shared_ingress = true
domain = "myapp.example.com"          # required: used as the Ingress host field

# Override if your shared controller uses a non-default class
# shared_ingress_class = "nginx"
```

**Non-nginx controllers (e.g. AWS ALB).** `shared_ingress_class` may name any controller; nginx-specific tuning is emitted only when the class is `nginx`. Supply controller-specific settings via `shared_ingress_annotations` so jac-scale stays cloud-agnostic:

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
shared_ingress = true
shared_ingress_class = "alb"
shared_ingress_tls = true
domain = "linkedin.jaseci.app"

[scale.kubernetes.shared_ingress_annotations]
"alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/group.name" = "shared-alb"     # join one shared ALB
"alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme" = "internet-facing"
"alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/target-type" = "ip"
"alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/certificate-arn" = "arn:aws:acm:...:certificate/..."
"alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/listen-ports" = '[{"HTTP": 80}, {"HTTPS": 443}]'
"alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect" = "443"
```

| Behaviour | Dedicated (default) | Shared |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Controller deployed | Yes (one per app) | No (uses existing controller) |
| IngressClass | `{namespace}-{app_name}-nginx` | Value of `shared_ingress_class` |
| Routing rules | Wildcard (host set by `--enable-tls`) | Host set immediately to `domain` |
| On destroy | Removes controller, RBAC, IngressClass, and Ingress rules | Removes Ingress rules only; controller is untouched |
| TLS (`--enable-tls`) | Works (cert-manager Issuer uses app-specific class) | Works (cert-manager Issuer uses shared class) |

!!! note
    Because the shared controller routes by the `Host:` header, each app in the cluster must have a unique domain. Two apps named `jaseci` in `dev` and `prod` namespaces are fully isolated as long as they have different domains (`dev.example.com` vs `prod.example.com`).

### Rate Limiting (DDoS Protection)

jac-scale applies NGINX rate limiting annotations to the Ingress to protect against abuse and DDoS traffic. Limits are enforced **per client IP**.

**How it works (leaky bucket algorithm):**

- **`ingress_limit_rps`** - sustained requests per second allowed per IP.
- **`ingress_limit_burst_multiplier`** - burst = `limit_rps x burst_multiplier`. Requests within the burst are queued; requests beyond it are dropped with `429`.
- **`ingress_limit_connections`** - maximum number of concurrent open connections per IP. Excess connections are rejected immediately.

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `ingress_limit_rps` | `20` | Sustained requests per second per client IP |
| `ingress_limit_burst_multiplier` | `5` | Burst headroom multiplier (burst = rps × multiplier) |
| `ingress_limit_connections` | `20` | Max concurrent connections per client IP |

Requests that exceed the limits receive `429 Too Many Requests`.

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
ingress_limit_rps = 50              # allow more sustained traffic
ingress_limit_burst_multiplier = 3  # tighter burst control
ingress_limit_connections = 30      # more concurrent connections
```

### Sticky Sessions (Cookie-Based Affinity)

When your pods hold per-user state (e.g. running user processes), you need requests from the same user to always reach the same pod. jac-scale supports opt-in cookie-based session affinity via NGINX.

**Enabled by default.** Disable it in `jac.toml` if not needed:

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
ingress_session_affinity = false
```

On the first response, NGINX sets a `route` cookie in the browser. Every subsequent request includes that cookie and NGINX uses it to route back to the same pod, regardless of IP changes (mobile networks, NAT, VPN, proxies). The cookie never expires in the browser.

| Behaviour | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| Cookie name | `route` |
| Cookie lifetime | Never expires (`max-age` ~68 years) |
| On pod failure | NGINX re-routes to a healthy pod and rewrites the cookie automatically |
| IP changes (mobile/NAT) | Handled correctly - routing is cookie-based, not IP-based |

- Your pods run stateful per-user processes (e.g. sandbox environments, background workers per user)
- You need a user to consistently land on the pod that owns their session

Sticky sessions ensure routing **while a pod is alive**. If a pod is deleted (e.g. during a rolling deployment), in-flight user processes on that pod are lost. The user is automatically re-routed to a new pod, but any in-memory state is gone. For true resilience, externalize per-user state to Redis or a database so any pod can serve any user.

### Domain & TLS (HTTPS)

jac-scale supports custom domain names and automatic HTTPS via [cert-manager](https://cert-manager.io) + Let's Encrypt. TLS is a two-step process to avoid the chicken-and-egg problem (NLB hostname is unknown until after the first deploy).

#### Step 1 - Deploy (HTTP)

Set your domain in `jac.toml` and deploy normally:

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
domain = "app.example.com"
cert_manager_email = "you@example.com"
```

```bash
jac start app.jac --scale
```

After deploy, the NLB hostname is printed:

```
Deployment complete! Service available at: http://k8s-default-...elb.amazonaws.com
Point your domain CNAME to: k8s-default-...elb.amazonaws.com
```

#### Step 2 - Add CNAME record

In your DNS registrar (Namecheap, Route 53, Cloudflare, etc.) add:

| Type | Host | Value |
|------|------|-------|
| CNAME | `app` (or `@`) | `k8s-default-...elb.amazonaws.com` |

Wait for DNS propagation (usually 1–15 minutes). Verify with `dig app.example.com`.

#### Step 3 - Enable TLS

```bash
jac start app.jac --scale --enable-tls
```

This installs cert-manager, creates a Let's Encrypt `Issuer`, patches the live Ingress with TLS annotations, and updates all service URLs to HTTPS. No redeployment of your application occurs.

```
TLS enabled. App is now live at:
  App URL:        https://app.example.com
  Grafana:        https://app.example.com/grafana
  Mongo Express:  https://app.example.com/db-dashboard
  RedisInsight:   https://app.example.com/cache-dashboard
```

> **Note:** `--enable-tls` requires `domain` to be set in `jac.toml`. It will error if no domain is configured.

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `domain` | `""` | Custom domain name (e.g. `app.example.com`). Leave empty for NLB-only access. |
| `cert_manager_email` | `""` | Email for Let's Encrypt certificate registration and expiry notices. |

**Certificate renewal** is automatic - cert-manager renews ~30 days before expiry.

### Resource Limits

Controls CPU and memory requests/limits for the application container. Kubernetes uses requests for scheduling and limits for enforcement (OOM-kill).

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `cpu_request`  | None | CPU units reserved for scheduling (e.g. `"250m"`) |
| `cpu_limit`  | None | Maximum CPU the container may use (e.g. `"1000m"`) |
| `memory_request`  | None | Memory reserved for scheduling (e.g. `"256Mi"`) |
| `memory_limit` | None | Memory ceiling - container is OOM-killed if exceeded |

Accepted suffixes: `Ki`, `Mi`, `Gi` (binary) or `K`, `M`, `G` (decimal).

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
cpu_request = "250m"
cpu_limit = "1000m"
memory_request = "256Mi"
memory_limit = "2Gi"
```

### Health Probes

Kubernetes uses readiness and liveness probes to decide when a pod is ready to serve traffic and when to restart it. Both probes hit `GET <health_check_path>` on the container.

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `health_check_path` | `"/docs"` | Endpoint probed by both readiness and liveness checks |
| `readiness_initial_delay` | `10` | Seconds to wait before first readiness check |
| `readiness_period` | `20` | Seconds between readiness checks |
| `liveness_initial_delay`  | `10` | Seconds to wait before first liveness check |
| `liveness_period`  | `20` | Seconds between liveness checks |
| `liveness_failure_threshold` | `80` | Consecutive failures before the pod is restarted |

```toml
[scale.kubernetes]
health_check_path = "/health"
readiness_initial_delay = 15
readiness_period = 10
liveness_initial_delay = 30
liveness_period = 30
liveness_failure_threshold = 5
```

> **Tip:** Set `health_check_path = "/health"` to use the built-in liveness and readiness endpoints - see [Health Checks](#health-checks).

### Autoscaling

jac-scale supports two autoscaler engines selected via `autoscaler_engine`. Both engines share `min_replicas`, `max_replicas`, and `cpu_utilization_target`; they differ in what additional triggers and behaviours they support.

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `autoscaler_engine` | `"hpa"` | Autoscaler engine: `"hpa"` (CPU/memory, default) or `"keda"` (event-driven, scale-to-zero) |
| `min_replicas` | `1` | Minimum number of pods |
| `max_replicas` | `3` | Maximum number of pods |
| `cpu_utilization_target` | `50` | Average CPU % that triggers scale-out. Seeds the CPU trigger for both engines unless explicitly overridden. |

> **Note:** CPU-based scaling requires `cpu_request` to be set. Without a CPU request, Kubernetes cannot compute a utilization percentage.

#### HPA Engine (Default)

The `"hpa"` engine creates a standard Kubernetes `HorizontalPodAutoscaler` that scales pods based on average CPU utilization.

#### KEDA Engine (Event-Driven Autoscaling)

The `"keda"` engine creates a `ScaledObject` custom resource instead of an HPA. It supports the full [KEDA trigger catalogue](https://keda.sh/docs/latest/scalers/) (Prometheus, Redis, RabbitMQ, Kafka, HTTP, and more) and enables scale-to-zero.

!!! note
    KEDA must be installed on the cluster before using this engine. If KEDA CRDs are absent at deploy time, jac-scale emits an install warning with a link to the [KEDA installation docs](https://keda.sh/docs/latest/deploy/) and falls back to static replicas rather than failing the deploy.

**Switching between engines is safe.** Each engine removes the other engine's resource (`ScaledObject` or `HPA`) on apply, so two autoscalers never compete for `spec.replicas` on the same Deployment.

!!! warning "CPU/memory triggers: scale-down always takes ~5 minutes"
    When using CPU or memory triggers, KEDA implements scaling through an internal Kubernetes `HorizontalPodAutoscaler`. Kubernetes applies a built-in 5-minute scale-down stabilization window (`stabilizationWindowSeconds = 300`) to every HPA regardless of the `autoscaler_cooldown` value set in `jac.toml`. Replicas will not decrease until CPU/memory has stayed below the target for a full 5 minutes. The `autoscaler_cooldown` setting is effective only for **event-driven triggers** (e.g. Prometheus, Redis, RabbitMQ) where KEDA directly controls the replica count without going through the HPA stabilization window.

!!! tip "Startup CPU spikes causing unwanted scale-up?"
    Pod initialization (Python imports, FastAPI startup, Jac runtime boot) can briefly spike CPU above the target, causing KEDA to scale up immediately after a fresh deploy before the app has finished starting. Set `autoscaler_initial_cooldown` to delay KEDA's first evaluation and give pods time to settle:
    ```toml
    autoscaler_initial_cooldown = 120  # wait 2 minutes after deploy before scaling
    ```

| TOML Key | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| `idle_replicas` | `null` | Replica count when all triggers go inactive. Set to `0` for scale-to-zero. Omit to fall back to `min_replicas`. |
| `autoscaler_polling_interval` | `30` | Seconds between trigger evaluations. |
| `autoscaler_cooldown` | `300` | Seconds of continuous inactivity before scaling down to `idle_replicas`. |
| `autoscaler_initial_cooldown` | `0` | Seconds after a fresh deploy before scale-to-zero becomes eligible. Prevents cold-start thrash on slow-booting apps. |
| `extra_triggers` | `[]` | Array of additional KEDA trigger tables applied to every service. See trigger entry keys below. |

| Key | Default | Description |
|-----|---------|-------------|
| `type` | (required) | KEDA trigger type (e.g. `"prometheus"`, `"redis"`, `"rabbitmq"`, `"kafka"`, `"http"`). See the [KEDA trigger catalogue](https://keda.sh/docs/latest/scalers/). |
| `metadata` | `{}` | Dict of trigger-specific key/value pairs. All values are coerced to strings before being sent to KEDA. |
| `name` | `null` | Optional label for this trigger in KEDA. When using `auth.secret_refs`, set a unique `name` per trigger; it is included in the hash that generates the `TriggerAuthentication` resource name (e.g. `order-service-daa02e

### Bug Fixes

- **Fix: client runtime never renders `[object Object]` for error envelopes**: the generated client runtime now unwraps the `{code, message, details}` error envelope to its message (mirroring how the success path unwraps `data`), so a failed `function`/`walker` call surfaces a readable string instead of a stringified object.
- **Fix: deterministic `jac gen-jir-registry` output**: The registry generator now runs its `jac format` step from a neutral working directory, so the generated `jir_registry.jac` is byte-identical regardless of the invoking project's `[check.lint]` settings. Previously, a project that enabled the opt-in deslop rules (`strip-comments`/`strip-docstrings`) would strip the docstrings from this generated file, breaking the `gen-jir-registry --verify` CI gate.
- **Fix: rt cache no longer collides across co-located checkouts**: The single-binary runtime cache (`~/.cache/jac/rt/`) was keyed only on the payload content hash, so two `jac` checkouts with byte-identical payloads shared one materialized tree, and the second checkout could silently execute the first's dev-linked `jaclang` source, masking per-branch differences during local testing. The cache dir is now `rt/<hash16>-<pathhash>`, folding in a digest of the binary's own path so each checkout gets its own tree; the shared version prefix still lets stale-tree GC reclaim previous payload versions without evicting co-located siblings.
- **Fix: ICE when `root`/`super` name a parameter**: Using the reserved refs `root` or `super` as a parameter name (in a `def`/`can` signature or a `lambda`) no longer crashes the compiler with an Internal Compiler Error in `PyastGenPass` ("Empty py_ast on ParamVar") or a bogus `W2003` "defined but never used". Because they lower to calls (`Jac.root()`/`super()`) they have no valid parameter form, so the parser now reports `E0013` ("keyword cannot be used as a parameter name") like any other keyword. Escape with a backtick (`` `root ``) to bind a local of that name. This also clears the precompile failure on the in-tree `jir.jac` (`scrub_cache_dirs`).
- **Fix: cross-module exception unwinding in the native backend**: A `raise` in one `.na.jac` module caught by a `try`/`except` in a different module aborted the process (SIGABRT) instead of unwinding to the handler. The setjmp/longjmp handler-stack head (`@__jac_current_handler`) is now a `weak` link-merged singleton shared across all linked modules, and exception type IDs are now stable and module-independent (derived from the canonical builtin hierarchy), so a cross-module raise reaches and matches its handler.
- **Fix: clean error for `root`/`super` in every binding position**: `root` and `super` lower to calls (`Jac.root()`/`super()`), so they cannot name a binding. Assignment and parameter names already reported a clear error, but `for root in ...`, comprehension targets, `has root: int`, `with ... as root`, `del root`, and `root := ...` previously leaked a cryptic backend `E5043`. These now all report `E0024` ("Cannot bind 'root' -- it is a built-in reference"), with backtick escaping (`` `root ``) remaining the documented way to bind a local of that name. The check is centralised in one validation-pass helper and recurses tuple/list destructuring; the bindable special names (`self`/`here`/`visitor`/`props`) are unaffected.
- **Fix: native `open(path, flags)` no longer ICEs the precompile**: A native module that calls the POSIX `open(path, flags_int)` syscall while its libc `open` extern is out of scope (e.g. a standalone bundled-payload precompile of a module whose imports live in a sibling file) no longer crashes the native backend with an uncaught `TypeError: Type of #2 arg mismatch: i8* != i64` from the LLVM IR builder. Because `open` is also the file-open builtin (`fopen(path, mode_str)`), an integer second argument was being forced through `fopen`; the backend now detects the non-string mode and emits the same clean `E5090` every other unlowerable native call gets. The file-open builtin (`open(path)`, `open(path, "r")`, `open(path, "rb")`) and a declared libc `open(path, flags) -> i32` extern are both unaffected. Clears the `✖ Error: FAIL` on `cef_platform.na.jac` (#7010 Error 2).
- **Fix: keyword arguments dropped in native calls**: On the native (`na`) backend, keyword arguments in a function or method call were silently dropped: the call fell back to the parameters' defaults instead of the values passed by keyword (so `greet(name="abcd", n=7)` ran the all-defaults body). Calls now bind keyword arguments to their named parameters (reordering as needed) across same-module, method, and cross-module (`.na.jac`) calls, matching the Python pathway.
- **Fix: native calls to bundled stdlib resolve against the bundled signature**: On the native (`na`) pathway, a call to a function or method imported from a bundled `na_stdlib` module was type-checked, keyword-bound, and default-filled against CPython's typeshed stub instead of the bundled `.na.jac` module that is actually linked and run. This broke modules whose signature diverges from CPython (keyword args bound to the wrong slot; omitted params filled from non-lowerable typeshed defaults). Native imports of bundled stdlib modules now resolve to the bundled `.na.jac` signature, so type-check, keyword binding, and default filling all use the linked module.
- **Fix: `nacompile` links bundled `na_stdlib` imports for auto-promoted `.jac` files**: a regular `.jac` (no `na {}` block) that imports a bundled native module is auto-promoted to the native backend, but `BoundaryAnalysisPass` only classified the import as a native cross-module import when the file was a `.na.jac` or the import sat in a `na {}` block. The auto-promote case skipped the cross-module IR merge, so the entry point called each module's `__jac_glob_init_<safe>` against an undefined symbol and the binary aborted at load. The auto-promote target is now treated as a native context, so the imported module's IR (and its initializers) is merged into the binary.
- **Fix: `SqliteMemory` `database is locked` under xdist write contention (#7056)**: `SqliteMemory._ensure_connection` set `journal_mode=WAL` + `synchronous=NORMAL` but never set `PRAGMA busy_timeout`, so a contending writer raised `sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked` immediately (0ms) instead of retrying. It now sets `PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000`, so contending writers retry for up to 5s before failing -- fixing the whole class of contention failures, not just the flaky `test_gcs_storage.jac` teardown that surfaced it. The Firebase CI step also now runs serial (`JAC_TEST_JOBS=0`), matching its sibling step, since all xdist workers share one relative `shelf_db_path` (`.jac/data/anchor_store.db`).

### Refactors

- **Config: single file-anchored `jac.toml` resolver**: Project-root resolution is now one canonical walk that every call site delegates to, and `jac format`/`jac check` lint config is resolved from the file being processed (nearest `jac.toml`, no ancestor merge) rather than the current working directory, so a file's rules no longer depend on where the command is run from, and an opinionated repo config (e.g. the deslop rules) never leaks onto files outside any project. Exclude-pattern matching is also unified into one shared helper with depth-agnostic path patterns.
- **Lint: Deslop core sources**: Stripped comments and docstrings from the core jaclang sources flagged by the repo-wide deslop gate (`strip-comments`/`strip-docstrings`, enabled in #7001), turning `Jac Type Check` green. Generated files and files whose comments are load-bearing (e.g. `jir_registry.jac`, `plugin_bridge.na.jac`, and source/location-coupled files asserted by langserve/runtime tests) are excluded via `[check.lint] exclude`.
- **Cleanup: Remove dead code and duplication across compiler core, runtime, and ecmascript**: Deleted grep-verified zero-caller code (the never-wired `primitives_py` Python emitter backend, dead ESTree records and unparser handlers, unreachable type-evaluator/property-as-`ClassType` clusters, dead runtime transports/utils/server forwarders, and assorted orphan helpers, fields, and imports) and de-duplicated repeated idioms into shared helpers (`ability_of_symbol`, `_collect_body_stmts`, `_run_function_with_occ`, `name_node`, `Parameter.clone`, `_spec_of`/`_typed_elem_llvm_of`, and the doc-IR/lint converter helpers). No behavior change.
- **Refactor: remove dead code in the native backend, jac0core, and project config** (part 2/2, companion to #7032): the llvmlite-port native LLVM IR backend drops ~1,000 lines of never-called machinery (32 unused `IRBuilder` methods plus their exclusive helper decorators, 14 orphaned instruction classes such as `InvokeInstr`/`SwitchInstr`/`CmpXchg`/`LandingPadInstr`, and the dead `declare_intrinsic` / `Type.intrinsic_name` cluster); jac0core sheds dead bootstrap helpers, unused `CompileOptions` fields, and the superseded JIR string-pool/varint serialization island; and `JacConfig.apply_cli_overrides` is removed. Behavior preserved -- emitted LLVM IR is byte-identical and every removal was verified dead across all liveness channels.
- **Cleanup: Remove dead planner filter cluster and dedup link-pass handlers**: Deleted the zero-caller `_CodeKey` object and `_filter_*_by_code` helpers in `runtimelib/planner.jac` (dead duplicates of the live copies in `query_utils.jac`) along with the imports they orphaned, and de-duplicated `PyJacAstLinkPass.exit_non_local_stmt`/`exit_global_stmt` by delegating the subclass handler to its base (`NonLocalStmt` subclasses `GlobalStmt`). No behavior change.

### New Features

- **Feature: the Jac-native desktop host runs on the fused single-binary runtime**: instead of binding the build machine's `libpython` by soname (`INSTSONAME` + an `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`/RUNPATH to its `LIBDIR`), a desktop app now brings up the **same fused runtime the `jac` CLI ships** -- the pinned, bundled CPython + jaclang carried in the binary's trailer payload. The CPython bring-up (materialize the payload → set a hermetic env → `dlopen` the bundled libpython → pin the program name → resolve the C-API) is unified in `launcher/embed.zig`, shared by the headless CLI launcher and the desktop host. The desktop host reaches it through a small C-ABI shim, `libjacpyembed` (`launcher/pyembed.zig`): the generated `host.na.jac` imports it by logical name (`import from jacpyembed`, resolved as a `$ORIGIN` sibling -- no `patchelf` for libpython), and `jac_engine_boot()` materializes the trailer and initializes the interpreter. The build appends this `jac`'s `[payload][trailer]` onto the compiled host, so it carries the bundled runtime and **auto-dedupes to the CLI's `rt/<hash>` cache** (identical payload hash). The shim ships inside the payload (`--pyembed`) and is staged `$ORIGIN`-adjacent at build time. Net effect: no build-machine libpython, no `INSTSONAME`, no `LD_LIBRARY_PATH`, and no per-build "bake the builder's site-packages into the binary" step -- the desktop app is the jac engine plus a window.
- **Feature: desktop bootstrap modules are imported from the fused runtime**: `oauth_broker`, `inprocess_dispatch`, and the desktop plugins are no longer `jac2py`-transpiled and staged as `.py` beside the binary -- the embedded interpreter imports them straight from the bundled jaclang tree (the plugins via their runtime dir on `sys.path`, with `import registry` cascading to every `built_in.*` plugin). Removes three staging passes and their `jac2py`/`jac0` subprocesses; only the app's own `sv` source tree and `dist/` bundle are still staged.
- **Feature: `na`/LLVM is first-class in the desktop runtime**: because the desktop app bundles the same payload the CLI ships -- which carries the LLVMPY shim and the native compiler -- an app's runtime can drive `jac nacompile` / `na` exactly like the CLI.
- **Feature: In-process `sv` dispatch in the Jac-native desktop host** (Phase J4), now running on the fused runtime: `cl` → `sv` walker/function calls on the desktop target run through the embedded CPython in-process instead of an HTTP POST to a remote backend. `inprocess_dispatch` boots the jac-scale ASGI app over a Starlette `TestClient` (no socket) and serves as the native `window.__jac_invoke` trampoline with the same `{kind, name, node, fields, token}` request contract and `{status, ok, body}` reply semantics as the web API. Dispatch runs on a single serialized worker thread off the GTK UI thread so a slow walker never freezes the window, with a configurable per-call timeout (`NATIVE_DISPATCH_TIMEOUT`, non-preemptive) and a bounded queue (`NATIVE_DISPATCH_BUSY` on saturation) set via `[plugins.desktop.dispatch]` (`timeout_s`, `max_queue`) in `jac.toml`. If the in-process backend fails to boot, the host shows a deterministic native error page instead of navigating to a silently-broken app, and `dispatch()` returns a `NATIVE_BOOT_ERROR` envelope rather than hanging. Native error envelopes follow a production error contract (`{code, message, request_id}`): every error carries a `request_id` for log correlation, and full tracebacks go to stderr only -- echoed into `error.details` solely when debug is enabled via `[plugins.desktop.dispatch].debug` (or the `JAC_DESKTOP_DEBUG` env var). Until jac-scale is bundled into the fused runtime, `sv` dispatch resolves jac-scale from a portable runtime seam (`JAC_DESKTOP_SCALE_SRC` / `JAC_DESKTOP_DEPS`), with no build-machine state baked in.
- **Feature: Native desktop IPC bridge for walker/function calls**: `__doWalkerFetch` and `__doFuncFetch` in the `cl` runtime route through `globalThis.__jac_invoke` when `__JAC_NATIVE__` is set (the Jac-native desktop shell), carrying the frozen `{kind, name, node, fields, token}` request and replaying HTTP status/body semantics locally, including 401 → re-login for walkers.
- **Feature: native `str(float)` now emits CPython's shortest round-trip repr (#6978 Phase 0.3)**: `str`/`repr`/`print`/f-string/`str.format` of a float on the native (na) backend previously used a lossy `%g` (`str(1.0)` gave "1" on na vs "1.0" on sv). A memoized `__jac_float_repr` helper now finds the smallest precision whose `%.*e` rendering re-parses (`strtod`) to the exact double, then reformats in CPython's `'r'` style (exponential when `decpt <= -4` or `decpt > 16`, else fixed `%.*f` with a trailing `.0` for integral values; nan/inf/-inf spelled as CPython does). This closes the `json.dumps` bare-float parity gap, and a new equivalence test pins na/sv byte-equality across 33 doubles including subnormals, the max double, `-0.0`, and inf/nan.
- **Native: upgrade to LLVM 22 and range-fetch LLVM via slices**: The native (`na`) backend now builds on LLVM 22.1.8 (the `LLVMPY_*` shim re-vendored verbatim from llvmlite 0.48.0rc1). The Linux shim links against GNU libstdc++ to match the official LLVM 22 Linux release (statically, so the shipped shim stays self-contained); macOS continues via Apple `clang++`/libc++. `zig build fetch-llvm` now range-fetches only the ~84 MB the shim links (`lib/libLLVM*.a` + `llvm`/`llvm-c` headers, plus macOS `libLTO.dylib`) from the `jaseci-labs/llvm-slice` repackaged zip instead of the ~1 GB upstream `.tar.xz`, with linked archives verified against a pinned manifest checksum.
- **Feature: native programs can static-link a bundled C floor into the binary (#6978 Phase 0.1a)**: a clib floor import such as `import from lzma { def lzma_crc32(...) }` now links the library's static archive directly into the `nacompile` binary instead of carrying a runtime dylib dependency. New `ar` archive reader in `linker_common` (`parse_ar_archive`, `parse_ar_symbol_index`, `select_ar_members`) pulls only the transitively-needed members; `MachOLinker` gained multi-object merge (`archive_members`) with cross-object symbol resolution; and `nacompile` resolves the import to its bundled `lib<name>.a`, dropping it from the dylib dependency list. Verified on macOS arm64 (the TLS-free floor set): `import from lzma`/`import from expat` programs run with `otool -L` showing only libSystem. Mach-O only for now; ELF/PE keep the dylib path, and production payload staging of the archives is Phase 0.2.
- **Feature: `jac test` accepts multiple file paths**: `jac test a.jac b.jac` previously errored with "unrecognized arguments"; the path argument is now a multi-value positional (matching `jac check`/`jac lint`), while bare `jac test` and single-file runs are unchanged.
- **Feature: native programs can static-link a bundled C floor into ELF binaries (#6978 Phase 0.1a)**: extends the static C-archive linking from #6986 (Mach-O) to ELF

### Breaking Changes

- **Breaking: Project kinds renamed to deliverable-oriented names**: The `jac create --kind` / `[project] kind` values have been renamed for consistency: `native-app`→`cli-native`, `shared-library`→`native-lib`, `api-service`→`service`, `microservices`→`service-mesh`, `pypi-package`→`py-package`, `npm-package`→`js-package`, `fullstack`→`web-app`, and `client`→`web-static` (`cli`, `native-binary`, `desktop`, `mobile` are unchanged). The client framework's built-in templates were renamed to match (`--use fullstack`→`--use web-app`, `--use client`→`--use web-static`). There are no back-compat aliases, so existing `jac.toml` files that set an old `kind` value (or scripts that pass an old `--kind`/`--use` name) must be updated.
- **Breaking: `jac-scale` folded into core as `jaclang.scale`**: The separate `jac-scale` PyPI package is gone (clean break). Scale (serve / deploy / microservices) is now a built-in plugin shipped inside the `jac` binary and registered automatically (no `jac install jac-scale`, no `jac plugins enable scale`). Imports move from `jac_scale.*` to `jaclang.scale.*`. Its optional heavy dependencies (fastapi, pymongo, redis, kubernetes, ...) are never bundled in the binary; they are pulled per-project into `.jac/venv` from `[scale.*]` config intent via the capability registry on `jac install`. `jac start` / `jac start --scale` and all `[scale.*]` / `[plugins.scale.*]` config are unchanged. `import jaclang` / `jac --version` import none of the scale runtime deps.

### New Features

- **Feature: Cloud storage config for Firebase/GCS**: `StorageConfig` gains a `firebase` storage type and a `project_id` setting, and the generalized cloud-storage options (bucket, prefix, region, endpoint) now cover GCS in addition to S3. The `Storage.delete` contract is documented to return whether a file was actually removed.
- **Feature: Archetype semantics registered in MTIR**: `MTIRGenPass` now records every archetype's `ClassInfo` (class semstr, `has`-fields, and abilities) in the MTIR map keyed `<module>.<ClassName>`, so the LLM runtime can describe walker/node/edge types (e.g. for `visit [-->] by llm` routing). Previously only `by llm` ability functions had MTIR entries, so walker/node attributes and ordinary `can ... entry` abilities were dropped. Also fixes a latent `ClassInfo` construction crash on the base-class fallback path and removes the now-dead `filter_visitable_by` reflection helpers. (#6767)
- **Feature: `jac x` runs installed CLI tools**: `jac x <tool>` runs an installed package's console-script command under the bundled runtime, resolving from the project venv first and the global site second.
- **Feature: `jac x` runs npm tools too**: `jac x <tool>` now also resolves the project's npm tools (`node_modules/.bin`) and runs them through the jac-managed bun runtime, searching the project Python venv, then project npm, then the global site.
- **Feature: bundled native `hashlib`/`hmac` over a static libcrypto floor, plus whole-module native imports (#6978 Phase 1)**: `import hashlib` / `import hmac` now resolve on the native (`nacompile`/JIT) pathway to bundled `na_stdlib` Mechanism-F modules, which are thin pure-Jac surfaces (`Hash`/`HMAC` objects with `update`/`digest`/`hexdigest`/`copy`, the per-algorithm constructors, and `hmac.new`) over OpenSSL's `libcrypto`, reached through an FFI floor (`_hashlib_native.na.jac`). Coverage is the always-built OpenSSL digests `md5`/`sha1`/`sha224`/`sha256`/`sha384`/`sha512` plus `sha3_256`/`sha3_512`; every `hexdigest` is held byte-identical to CPython by a tri-backend equivalence test, and an `nacompile`d binary statically links the bundled `libcrypto.a` (no `libcrypto.so` dependency). This also adds general whole-module imports of bundled `.na.jac` stdlib modules on the native pathway: `import hashlib; hashlib.sha256(...)` now links the module and routes the module-qualified call to its function exactly like the selective `import from hashlib { sha256 }` form (previously only the selective form and a hardcoded `math`/`os`/`sys`/`time`/`random`/`struct` list were accepted). Supporting native-codegen fixes land alongside: a type-correct default-argument fallback (a bare `sha256()` no longer emits an invalid `i32 0` for a `bytes` default, E5020), `:priv` internal linkage on `hashlib`'s internal `new` so it no longer collides with `hmac`'s public `new` at link time (E5026), and ELF static-archive floor-linking fixes (local-symbol namespacing, `.bss`/NOBITS plus COMMON allocation, a local-GOT region, `atexit`/`__dso_handle`, and per-section base alignment).
- **Bundle bun into the runtime**: bun is now a contained part of the jac runtime, acquired once at build time, sha256-verified, bundled inside the binary, and resolved by absolute path. It is no longer downloaded from GitHub at runtime or placed on the user's `PATH`, mirroring how the bundled CPython is hermetic. Editable/source checkouts place it with `zig build fetch-bun`.
- **Feature: opt-in deslop lint rules + `jac precommit`**: Adds two destructive, opt-in auto-lint rules, `strip-comments` (W3050) and `strip-docstrings` (W3051), that remove all comments/docstrings only when named explicitly in `[check.lint]` select (never under `all`/`default`), plus a `jac precommit` command that runs `jac format --lintfix` and `jac check` using `jac.toml` settings and can install a git pre-commit hook.
- **Feature: bundled native `secrets` over a libcrypto CSPRNG floor (#6978 Phase 1)**: `import secrets` now resolves on the native (`nacompile`/JIT) pathway to a bundled `na_stdlib` Mechanism-F module, a thin pure-Jac surface (`secrets.na.jac`) over an OpenSSL `RAND_bytes` FFI floor (`_csprng_native.na.jac`) on the same bundled `libcrypto.a` the `hashlib`/`hmac` modules link. `RAND_bytes` is seeded from the OS CSPRNG, the same source CPython's `secrets` draws from via `os.urandom`. Coverage is `token_bytes`, `token_hex`, `token_urlsafe` (base64-url, padding stripped, exactly as CPython renders), and `randbelow` (uniform via rejection sampling, matching CPython's `SystemRandom._randbelow`); a `RAND_bytes` failure raises `ValueError` rather than returning the zero buffer, and an `nacompile`d binary statically links `libcrypto.a` and runs self-contained. A new tri-backend equivalence test (`prim_secrets`) pins the native and CPython implementations congruent on the deterministic properties of the random output (token lengths including the empty case, `randbelow(1) == 0`, the in-range bound, and that two draws differ).
- **Native: `__str__` dispatch for `str()` / `print()` / f-strings**: On the native (`na`) pathway, `str(obj)`, `print(obj)`, and `f"{obj}"` now dispatch to a user object's `__str__` (resolving the runtime subclass through the vtable) instead of rendering the raw struct pointer as garbage, and fall back to a `<TypeName object at 0x...>` default when no `__str__` is defined.
- **Feature: bundled native `uuid` (v4) on the libcrypto CSPRNG floor (#6978 Phase 1)**: `import uuid` now resolves on the native (`nacompile`/JIT) pathway to a bundled `na_stdlib` Mechanism-F module (`uuid.na.jac`) over the same OpenSSL `RAND_bytes` floor (`_csprng_native.na.jac`) that `secrets` uses. `uuid4()` draws 16 CSPRNG bytes and renders the 32-char `hex`, applying the v4 version (byte 6 high nibble) and RFC-4122 variant (byte 8) bits during the render (native `bytes` are immutable, so the bits are applied at render time); `UUID.hex`, `str(UUID)` (the canonical `8-4-4-4-12` form, via native `__str__` dispatch from #7005), and `UUID.version` match CPython's API, and an `nacompile`d binary statically links `libcrypto.a` and runs self-contained. A new tri-backend equivalence test (`prim_uuid`) pins the native and CPython implementations congruent on the deterministic v4 structure of the random output (hex/str lengths, hyphen layout, version nibble, RFC-4122 variant nibble, `version == 4`, and that two draws differ). The `.bytes`/`.int`/`.urn` attributes (which need a constructed `bytes`) are follow-ups.
- **Feature: Dev-mode notice in the CLI**: When the editable dev loop is active (`[dev] jaclang_source` in the nearest `jac.toml`, or a linked `-Ddev` binary), the `jac` CLI now prints a one-line notice on stderr identifying the in-repo compiler source it is running against, so it is never a surprise that the bundled compiler is being bypassed.
- **Feature: Capability registry for scale dependencies**: Core jaclang gains a single-source-of-truth capability registry (`jaclang.project.capabilities`) that maps each scale capability (`serve`, `data.mongo`, `deploy.k8s`, ...) to its pinned pip deps and a `runtime`/`deploy` context. Capabilities are derived from `[scale]` config intent and the command being run, then resolved into two context-split closures so a runtime pod never carries deploy-only clients like `kubernetes`/`docker`. `DependencyInstaller.sync_capabilities` installs a resolved closure into the project `.jac/venv`.
- **Built-in MCP server with zero external dependencies**: The `jac mcp` Model Context Protocol server is now part of the `jac` binary instead of the separate `jac-mcp` PyPI plugin. The protocol (JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, streamable-HTTP, and SSE) is reimplemented on the Python standard library, so the server no longer pulls in the `mcp` SDK, pydantic, starlette, uvicorn, httpx, or cryptography. Run `jac mcp` directly with no install step.
- **Feature: Curated bare `jac` help screen**: Running `jac` with no arguments now shows the Jac logo plus a curated set of common commands and a "Learn & build with AI" section that funnels users (and AI agents) to `jac guide`, instead of dumping every command. The full grouped command catalog moves to `jac --help`, which now renders through the styled formatter. Commands opt into the curated screen via a new data-driven `tier` field on `CommandSpec`.
- **Feature: bundled native `ssl` (verifying TLS client) over a libssl/libcrypto floor (#6978 Phase 1)**: `import ssl` now resolves on the native (`nacompile`/JIT) pathway to a bundled `na_stdlib` module -- a pure-Jac verifying-TLS-client surface (`ssl.na.jac`) over an OpenSSL `libssl`/`libcrypto` FFI floor (`_ssl_native.na.jac`, reusing the same bundled `libcrypto` the `hashlib`/`secrets` floors link). The verification posture is CPython's, never hand-rolled: the context is created with `SSL_VERIFY_PEER`, a CA bundle is loaded (the system / `SSL_CERT_FILE` store by default, or an explicit `cafile`), SNI is sent, and the peer hostname is pinned with `SSL_set1_host`, so the handshake fails closed on an untrusted chain OR a hostname mismatch and the surface raises a catchable `SSLError`. Surface: `SSLError`, `SSLContext`, `create_default_context`, and `SSLContext.wrap_socket(sock, server_hostname=...)` returning an `SSLSocket` with `send`/`sendall`/`recv`/`close`. A new equivalence test (`test_ssl_equivalence`) stands up a loopback HTTPS server with a test-generated self-signed cert and asserts, in CI, that a trusted cert + correct hostname verifies and GETs 200 (congruent with CPython), while an untrusted chain and a wrong hostname each fail closed with `SSLError`. A single `SSLError` type is used (not CPython's `SSLCertVerificationError` subclass) pending native base-class `except` matching.
- **Feature: native `urllib.request.urlopen` reimplemented over the `socket`+`ssl` floors; libcurl floor removed (#6978 Phase 1)**: the bundled `na_stdlib/urllib/request.na.jac` is now a pure-Jac surface over the bundled `socket` and `ssl` modules -- it parses the URL, opens the connection (TLS-wrapped and certificate/hostname-verified for `https`), sends an HTTP/1.0 GET, drains the response, splits headers from body, and follows redirects -- replacing the previous libcurl FFI floor. A `nacompile`d program that uses `urlopen` now links no foreign C beyond libc/libssl/libcrypto (**no `libcurl` dependency**, removing the last non-pbs C dependency and the only user-facing variadic-FFI caller). The `urlopen(url) -> HTTPResponse` surface (`status`/`body_length`/`reason`/`url`/`body`/`header_names`/`header_values`) is preserved, and `test_urllib_equivalence` keeps pinning it congruent with CPython's `urllib.request` against a loopback server for a direct GET and a 301 redirect.
- **Feature: bundled native `socket` (TCP client) over a libc BSD-sockets floor (#6978 Phase 1)**: `import socket` resolves on the native (`nacompile`/JIT) pathway to a bundled `na_stdlib` module -- a pure-Jac TCP-client surface (`socket.na.jac`) over a thin libc FFI floor (`_socket_native.na.jac`). Unlike the `hashlib`/`secrets` floors that statically link a bundled `libcrypto.a`, the socket primitives are plain BSD sockets in libc (`socket`/`connect`/`send`/`recv`/`getaddrinfo`), so a socket program links nothing beyond libc/libm and is self-contained with no bundled archive -- and no libcurl. Coverage is a blocking TCP client: the `socket(family, type, proto)` object with `connect`/`send`/`sendall`/`recv`/`close`/`fileno`, the family-spanning `create_connection((host, port))` helper that the `ssl` wrapper and `http`/`urllib` build on, and the `AF_*`/`SOCK_*` constants; setup and transport failures raise a catchable `OSError`. A new equivalence suite (`test_socket_equivalence`) pins the native client congruent with CPython's `socket` against a loopback server (HTTP status + exact byte count) and asserts that connecting to a closed port raises a catchable `OSError`. This is the transport `ssl` and `http`/`urllib` are built on (#7052). DNS resolution walks `getaddrinfo`'s `addrinfo` list with no new compiler intrinsic -- a libc `memcpy` whose source pointer is carried as a plain Jac `int` (the `FILE*`-as-`int` FFI convention) copies the sockaddr out and `int.from_bytes` reads the fields.

### Bug Fixes

- **Fix: client runtime never renders `[object Object]` for error envelopes**: the generated client runtime now unwraps the `{code, message, details}` error envelope to its message (mirroring how the success path unwraps `data`), so a failed `function`/`walker` call surfaces a readable string instead of a stringified object.
- **Fix: deterministic `jac gen-jir-registry` output**: The registry generator now runs its `jac format` step from a neutral working directory, so the generated `jir_registry.jac` is byte-identical regardless of the invoking project's `[check.lint]` settings. Previously, a project that enabled the opt-in deslop rules (`strip-comments`/`strip-docstrings`) would strip the docstrings from this generated file, breaking the `gen-jir-registry --verify` CI gate.
- **Fix: rt cache no longer collides across co-located checkouts**: The single-binary runtime cache (`~/.cache/jac/rt/`) was keyed only on the payload content hash, so two `jac` checkouts with byte-identical payloads shared one materialized tree, and the second checkout could silently execute the first's dev-linked `jaclang` source, masking per-branch differences during local testing. The cache dir is now `rt/<hash16>-<pathhash>`, folding in a digest of the binary's own path so each checkout gets its own tree; the shared version prefix still lets stale-tree GC reclaim previous payload versions without evicting co-located siblings.
- **Fix: ICE when `root`/`super` name a parameter**: Using the reserved refs `root` or `super` as a parameter name (in a `def`/`can` signature or a `lambda`) no longer crashes the compiler with an Internal Compiler Error in `PyastGenPass` ("Empty py_ast on ParamVar") or a bogus `W2003` "defined but never used". Because they lower to calls (`Jac.root()`/`super()`) they have no valid parameter form, so the parser now reports `E0013` ("keyword cannot be used as a parameter name") like any other keyword. Escape with a

| Rule Name | Code | Description | Group | |-----------|------|-------------|-------| | `staticmethod-to-static` | `W3001` | Convert `@staticmethod` decorator to `static` keyword | default | | `combine-has` | `W3002` | Combine consecutive `has` statements with same modifiers | default | | `combine-glob` | `W3003` | Combine consecutive `glob` statements with same modifiers | default | | `init-to-can` | `W3004` | Convert `def __init__` / `def __post_init__` to `can init` / `can postinit` | default | | `remove-empty-parens` | `W3005` | Remove empty parentheses from declarations (`def foo()` → `def foo`) | default | | `remove-kwesc` | `W3006` | Remove unnecessary backtick escaping from non-keyword names | default | | `hasattr-to-null-ok` | `W3007` | Convert `hasattr(obj, "attr")` to null-safe access (`obj?.attr`) | default | | `simplify-ternary` | `W3008` | Simplify `x if x else default` to `x or default` | default | | `remove-future-annotations` | `W3009` | Remove `import from __future__ { annotations }` (not needed in Jac) | default | | `fix-impl-signature` | `W3010` | Fix signature mismatches between declarations and implementations | default | | `remove-import-semi` | `W3011` | Remove trailing semicolons from `import from X { ... }` | default | | `no-print` | `E3012` | Error on bare `print()` calls (use console abstraction instead) | all | | `strip-comments` | `W3050` | Remove **all** comments | opt-in | | `strip-docstrings` | `W3051` | Remove **all** docstrings | opt-in | Diagnostic codes can be suppressed inline with `# jac:ignore[CODE]` comments. See the full [Errors & Warnings](../diagnostics.md) reference for all diagnostic codes. `strip-comments` and `strip-docstrings` are destructive "deslop" rules: they delete content rather than restructure it. Unlike every other rule, they are **never** activated by `select = ["all"]` or `select = ["default"]`; they fire only when named explicitly. A project that wants them on by default lists them alongside its other selections: ```toml [check.lint] select = ["default", "strip-comments", "strip-docstrings"] ``` The two are independent, so you can strip comments while keeping docstrings (or vice versa). With a rule selected, `jac fmt --lintfix` removes the content and `jac check` reports it. They are also the rules driving [`jac precommit`](../cli/index.md#jac-precommit) when configured. Use `exclude` to skip files matching glob patterns: ```toml [check.lint] select = ["all"] exclude = [ "docs/*", "*/examples/*", "*/tests/*", "legacy_module.jac", ] ``` Patterns are matched against file paths relative to the project root. Use `*` for single-directory wildcards and `**` for recursive matching. ### [dot] Defaults for `jac dot` (graph visualization): ```toml [dot] depth = -1 # Traversal depth (-1 = unlimited) traverse = false # Traverse connections bfs = false # Use BFS (default: DFS) edge_limit = 512 # Maximum edges node_limit = 512 # Maximum nodes format = "dot" # Output format ``` ### [cache] Bytecode cache settings: ```toml [cache] enabled = true # Enable caching dir = "cache" # Cache subdirectory under the build dir (i.e. .jac/cache). # An absolute path relocates the cache wholesale. ``` The format cache (`jac fmt --cache` / `jac precommit`) also lives here, under `/fmt-v1/`. It stores one marker per file proven clean, keyed on the file's content digest, the `lintfix` mode, the effective `[format]` and `[check]` settings (including `suppress` / `suppress_categories` / nested `lint`), the logical path when `lintfix` is on, and a formatter-pipeline fingerprint -- so a content, config, path, or pipeline change automatically invalidates the relevant entries. Entries are written only for fully successful, unchanged (or just-rewritten) results; syntax errors, lint failures, and annex failures are never cached as clean. `--cache` / precommit enable this format cache explicitly and do **not** consult `[cache].enabled` (that flag gates the bytecode cache). The directory is git-ignored, so it is safe to delete at any time. See [`jac fmt --cache`](../cli/index.md#jac-fmt). ### [storage] !!! note "Scale Configuration" The `[storage]` section is provided by the built-in **scale** subsystem (part of `jaclang` core). Its cloud backends (S3/GCS/Azure) require the relevant client libraries in the project venv -- declare the backend in config and run `jac install` to pull them in. ```toml [storage] storage_type = "local" # Storage backend (local) base_path = "./storage" # Base directory for files create_dirs = true # Auto-create directories ``` | Field | Description | Default | |-------|-------------|---------| | `storage_type` | Storage backend type | `"local"` | | `base_path` | Base directory for file storage | `"./storage"` | | `create_dirs` | Automatically create directories | `true` | | Variable | Description | |----------|-------------| | `JAC_STORAGE_TYPE` | Storage type (overrides config) | | `JAC_STORAGE_PATH` | Base directory (overrides config) | | `JAC_STORAGE_CREATE_DIRS` | Auto-create directories (`"true"`/`"false"`) | Configuration priority: `jac.toml` > environment variables > defaults. ### Capability settings Built-in capabilities (byLLM, scale, the client framework) are configured in top-level tables named after the capability: ```toml # byLLM settings (model identity split from call params) [byllm.model] default_model = "gpt-4o" api_key = "${OPENAI_API_KEY}" [byllm.call_params] temperature = 0.7 # Server settings (scale) [scale.server] port = 8000 host = "0.0.0.0" docs_enabled = true # Set to false to disable /docs, /redoc, /openapi.json # Webhook settings (scale) [scale.webhook] secret = "your-webhook-secret-key" signature_header = "X-Webhook-Signature" verify_signature = true api_key_expiry_days = 365 # Kubernetes version pinning (scale) -- scale, byLLM, the MCP server, and the # client/desktop framework all ship inside the `jac` binary, so they need no # pinning. See [Built-in Local Models](../plugins/byllm.md#built-in-local-models) for the full reference and [`jac model`](../cli/index.md#jac-model) for cache management. ```toml [client] framework = "react" # "react" (default), "solid" (experimental), or "preact" ``` Controls which JavaScript framework the `cl` compiler target emits. String values must be double-quoted (JSON-encoded). In client code, access them directly: ```jac cl { def:pub Footer() -> JsxElement { return

; } } ``` ### [scripts] Custom command shortcuts: ```toml [scripts] dev = "jac run main.jac" test = "jac test -v" build = "jac build main.jac -t" lint = "jac check . --lint --fix" format = "jac fmt ." ``` Run with: ```bash jac x dev jac x test ``` ### [environments] Environment-specific overrides: ```toml [environment] default_profile = "development" [environments.development] [environments.development.run] cache = false [environments.development.byllm] model = "gpt-3.5-turbo" [environments.production] inherits = "development" [environments.production.run] cache = true [environments.production.byllm] model = "gpt-4" ``` Activate a profile: ```bash JAC_PROFILE=production jac run main.jac ``` ## Environment Variable Interpolation Use environment variable interpolation inside `jac.toml` values: ```toml [byllm.model] api_key = "${OPENAI_API_KEY}" # Required default_model = "${MODEL:-gpt-4o-mini}" # With default base_url = "${BASE_URL:?Base URL is required}" # Required with error ``` | Syntax | Description | |--------|-------------| | `${VAR}` | Use variable (error if not set) | | `${VAR:-default}` | Use default if not set | | `${VAR:?error}` | Custom error if not set | ### [project.include] Controls which files and directories `jac build --as wheel` collects into the wheel. > **Note:** Earlier releases used a separate `[package]` / `[package.include]` section for publishing metadata. As of jaclang 0.15, `[package]` has been merged into `[project]` -- all publishing fields now live under `[project]` (see above), and file-inclusion rules live under `[project.include]`. Plain `[package]` tables are no longer read. ```toml [project.include] # Explicit list of package directories to include. # Defaults to a directory matching the package name (hyphens replaced with underscores). packages = ["mylib", "mylib_utils"] [project.include.data] # "*" sets global file patterns for all packages. "*" = ["**/*.jac", "**/*.py", "**/*.pyi", "py.typed"] # Per-package overrides add extra patterns on top of globals. mylib = ["**/*.lark", "data/*.json", "templates/**/*"] ``` | Key | Description | |-----|-------------| | `packages` | Glob list of package directories to ship. Defaults to one directory named after the project (hyphens → underscores). | | `data` | Map of file-glob patterns. The `"*"` key sets global patterns for every package; a per-package key adds extra patterns on top. | Simple patterns without a path separator (e.g. `"*.jac"`) are matched recursively, so sub-packages are covered automatically. **Default included patterns** (when `[project.include.data]` is absent): | Pattern | Description | |---------|-------------| | `**/*.jac` | Jac source files | | `**/*.py` | Python source files | | `**/*.pyi` | Type stub files | | `**/*.lark` | Lark grammar files | | `**/py.typed` | PEP 561 type marker | | `**/*.jir` | Pre-compiled JIR bytecode (collected if already present -- see [`jac build`](../cli/index.md#jac-build)) | | `_precompiled/manifest.json` | JIR precompile manifest | **Always excluded** (regardless of patterns): - Directories: `.jac/`, `__pycache__/`, `dist/`, `build/`, `venv/`, `.venv/`, `env/`, `.git/`, `.hg/`, `node_modules/`, `*.egg-info/` - File suffixes: `.pyc` ### [entrypoints] Declare console scripts and other entry-point groups. Maps directly to `entry_points.txt` in the wheel's `.dist-info`. ```toml [entrypoints.scripts] # Installs a "mylib" CLI command pointing to mylib.cli:main mylib = "mylib.cli:main" ``` The `[entrypoints.scripts]` group is written as `[console_scripts]` in `entry_points.txt`, which is the standard pip convention for installing CLI commands. After a user runs `pip install mylib`, the `mylib` command is available on their `PATH`. Any other `[entrypoints.]` table is written through to the wheel metadata verbatim, for consumers that discover packages via `importlib.metadata.entry_points()`. (Jac itself no longer loads any entry-point group at startup -- the former `jac` plugin group is defunct.) ## CLI Override Most settings can be overridden via CLI flags: ```bash # Override run settings jac run --no-cache main.jac # Override test settings jac test --verbose -x # Override serve settings jac start --port 3000 ``` ## Complete Example ```toml [project] name = "my-ai-app" version = "1.0.0" description = "An AI-powered application" entry-point = "main.jac" [dependencies] byllm = ">=0.4.8" requests = ">=2.28.0" [dev-dependencies] pytest = ">=8.0.0" [run] main = true cache = true topology_index = true [serve] port = 8000 cl_route_prefix = "cl" [test] directory = "tests" verbose = true [build] typecheck = true dir = ".jac" [check.lint] select = ["all"] ignore = [] exclude = [] [byllm.model] default_model = "${LLM_MODEL:-gpt-4o-mini}" api_key = "${OPENAI_API_KEY}" [scripts] dev = "jac run main.jac" test = "jac test" lint = "jac check . --lint --fix" ``` ## .jacignore The `.jacignore` file controls which Jac files are excluded from compilation and analysis. Place it in the project root. ### Format One pattern per line, similar to `.gitignore`: ``` # Comments start with # vite_client_bundle.impl.jac test_fixtures/ *.generated.jac ``` Each line is a filename or pattern that should be skipped during Jac compilation passes (type checking, formatting, etc.). ## Environment Variables ### General | Variable | Description | |----------|-------------| | `NO_COLOR` | Disable colored terminal output | | `NO_EMOJI` | Disable emoji in terminal output | | `JAC_PROFILE` | Activate a configuration profile (e.g., `production`) | | `JAC_BASE_PATH` | Override base directory for data/storage | | `JAC_DATA_PATH` | Override the base directory for application data (graph storage, user db) | | `JACPATH` | Colon-separated extra search path for Jac module resolution (like `PYTHONPATH`) | | `JAC_SCHEMA_REPAIR` | Schema-drift handling on load: `repair` (default) or `strict` | | `JAC_ST

## Shared Libraries (C ABI)

Where `import from "lib.so" { ... }` lets native Jac *call into* C libraries, `jac nacompile --shared` does the inverse: it packages a `.na.jac` module as a **C-ABI shared library** that any C/C++/Python/Rust host can load and call. The same self-contained pipeline emits an ELF `.so`, a Mach-O `.dylib`, or a PE `.dll` -- no system linker required.

```bash
jac nacompile mathlib.na.jac --shared          # -> ./libmathlib.so   (host platform)
jac nacompile mathlib.na.jac --shared --target macos     # -> ./libmathlib.dylib
jac nacompile mathlib.na.jac --shared --target windows   # -> ./libmathlib.dll
```

### Choosing what to export

A shared library has no `with entry {}` -- its surface is whatever you mark **`:pub`**. Only explicitly `:pub` functions and globals are placed in the library's export table; everything else stays internal (callable *within* the library, invisible to a host). This makes `:pub` a curated C-ABI surface rather than a dump of every symbol.

```jac
# mathlib.na.jac
glob:pub counter: int = 7;          # exported global (read via dlsym/GetProcAddress)

def:pub jadd(a: int, b: int) -> int {   # exported function
    return a + b;
}

def helper(x: int) -> int {          # NOT exported -- internal only
    return x * 2;
}

obj:pub Point {
    has x: int = 0, y: int = 0;
}

def:pub make_point(x: int, y: int) -> Point {
    return Point(x=x, y=y);          # returns an opaque handle (see below)
}

def:pub point_sum(p: Point) -> int {
    return p.x + p.y;
}
```

`:pub` symbols exported from imported native modules are re-exported too, so a library can be composed from several `.na.jac` files.

### Calling it from C

The exported names are plain C symbols. Scalars (`int`->`int64`, `float`->`double`, `bool`) pass by value; the library links and loads with the standard toolchain:

```c
// gcc app.c -L. -lmathlib -Wl,-rpath,. -o app
extern long jadd(long, long);
extern long get_counter(void);
int main(void) { return (int)(jadd(2, 3) + get_counter()); }  // 12
```

```python
import ctypes
lib = ctypes.CDLL("./libmathlib.so")
lib.jadd.restype = ctypes.c_int64
lib.jadd.argtypes = [ctypes.c_int64, ctypes.c_int64]
print(lib.jadd(2, 3))   # 5
```

### Opaque object handles and lifetimes

Jac objects, strings, lists and dicts are reference-counted heap values. They cross the C ABI as **opaque handles** (`void*`): a host receives the pointer from one `:pub` function and passes it to another, but must not dereference it directly. Because the library manages those objects with reference counting, it also exports two helpers so a host can manage their lifetime:

```c
void  jac_retain(void *handle);    // take a reference
void  jac_release(void *handle);   // drop a reference (frees at zero)
```

```python
p = lib.make_point(3, 4)     # opaque Point*
lib.point_sum(p)             # -> 7
lib.jac_release(p)           # release when done
```

### Initialization

Module globals are initialized automatically when the library is loaded -- there is no `jac_init()` to remember. The loader runs an injected `__jac_shared_init` via the platform's standard mechanism (ELF `DT_INIT_ARRAY`, Mach-O `__mod_init_func`, PE `DllMain` on `DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH`), which runs this module's and every imported native module's global initializers before any export is called.

### Per-platform output

| Target | Output | Format details |
|--------|--------|----------------|
| Linux (default/host) | `lib<name>.so` | `ET_DYN`, PIC, exported `.dynsym` + `.hash`, `R_*_RELATIVE` fixups, `DT_INIT_ARRAY`, section headers (so `ld -l`, `readelf`, `nm -D` all work) |
| `--target macos` | `lib<name>.dylib` | `MH_DYLIB`, export trie, `__mod_init_func`, `LC_ID_DYLIB`; ad-hoc code-signed on arm64 |
| `--target windows` | `lib<name>.dll` | `IMAGE_FILE_DLL`, export directory, `.reloc` base relocations, `DllMain` entry |

!!! note "What can be exported"
    A `:pub` export's parameters and return value must be C-ABI representable: scalars and pointers (Jac objects/strings/containers as opaque `void*` handles), plus the C struct types from `import from "lib"` interop. Methods are not exported (their symbol is class-qualified, not a valid C name) -- wrap them in a `:pub` free function.

### Primitive Type Mappings

Native compilation maps Jac types to LLVM types:

| Jac Type | LLVM Type | Size | Notes |
|----------|-----------|------|-------|
| `int` | `i64` | 8 bytes | 64-bit signed integer |
| `float` | `f64` | 8 bytes | 64-bit double precision |
| `bool` | `i1` | 1 bit | Boolean |
| `str` | `i8*` | pointer | Null-terminated byte string |
| `None` | -- | -- | Null pointer |

### Fixed-Width Types

For C interop and precise control, Jac provides fixed-width integer and float types:

| Type | Size | Description |
|------|------|-------------|
| `i8` / `u8` | 1 byte | Signed / unsigned 8-bit |
| `i16` / `u16` | 2 bytes | Signed / unsigned 16-bit |
| `i32` / `u32` | 4 bytes | Signed / unsigned 32-bit |
| `i64` / `u64` | 8 bytes | Signed / unsigned 64-bit |
| `f32` | 4 bytes | Single-precision float |
| `f64` | 8 bytes | Double-precision float |
| `c_void` | pointer | Opaque pointer (for C interop) |

### Collection Internals

Collections are represented as LLVM struct types:

| Jac Type | Internal Layout |
|----------|----------------|
| `list[T]` | `{ i64 capacity, i64 len, T* data }` |
| `dict[K, V]` | `{ K* keys, V* values, i64 len }` |
| `set[T]` | `{ T* data, i64 len }` |
| `tuple[T, ...]` | LLVM literal struct (fields packed by type) |
